**PART 30: SOME LESSONS I LEARNT**

**The Tongue**  
What is the most important organ of human life? Obviously the heart which pumps blood more than 100,000 times a day, which means if you live for 75 years, it will pump approx. 2.75 billion times. Most miraculously, it pumps on its own; you don’t do anything for it to pump, except for taking care of it sometimes with good food and aerobic exercise. With the blood being pumped by the heart, all other organs play their vital role in their own domains.

But what is the most important organ for living a good life? I say “the tongue”. Tongue is the only organ for the sense of taste and unhealthy but tasty food destroys our health. Food is a vital factor of our health other than some other lifestyle habits. So our tongue is responsible for our physical health. 

Also we can speak because of our tongue. The way we speak determines our character and this character determines your relationship, be it with your family or friends or relatives or neighbours or colleagues in the corporate. The way politicians speak also determines his relationship with the public because politicians are public figures. So tongue is the main organ for your relationships. It is said that deaf and dumb do not have enemies; yes, they may have the tongue but cannot speak. 

**Lesson**: The tongue is responsible for two major aspects in one’s life; your physical health and your relationships. Watch your tongue from now on.  
\--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- 

**True Friends**  
Today I am living my third life. I was returning from a seminar from an outstation place to my house. All my juniors were also returning by a bus. The seniors were returning in their own cars. The cars were ahead and the bus was following us. My driver was relatively new on the highway. He put the brakes of my car much before; almost 10 feet before a truck suddenly appeared, not realizing that a heavy 50 ton trailer was coming from behind. As soon as the car stopped, the trailer banged my car from behind and it went flying for quite a distance. The windscreen behind was shattered and the broken glass scattered inside the car. There was a spare wheel kept at the back of my car on which the trailer actually banged. Maybe the wheel absorbed most of the impact. Even if the broken glass splintered, I did not get single scratch on my forehead or eyes. I was not even injured. The driver was slightly injured. I was fine physically but shocked with trauma. I went inside the police station which was just besides the accident spot.

All my colleagues, some them perceived friends, alighted from the bus coming from behind and came to meet me in the police station. Astonishingly I observed that almost all barring three of them consoled me but left for their homes citing important personal works. The three of them were all along with me helping me to talk to the police, file FIR, arrange a towing vehicle to take my car to the workshop and arrange another sedan car to take me home comfortably. **That day I realized who my true friends were**\!

**Lesson**: You may have many friends flocking around you even in the corporate world. But be careful, most of them are perceived to be friends, only some are true friends. It’s possible to identify true friends at times of distress only.  
\--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- 

**Health is Wealth**  
As I started leading a second life, sometime later in life, I encountered chest pain occasionally. In the beginning I just thought them to be gastric pains and I presumed that the antacids were working. However, on a severe unbearable chest pain one day, I had to be hospitalized. The doctor smiled and was casual; I was annoyed a bit and asked the doctor “I had terrible chest pain and have come to consult you, but it seems you are smiling and taking it casually”. The doctor replied “my dear friend, do you know how lucky you are? Do you know how many patients come here brought dead? At least by the grace of God, you are still living and after necessary interventions you will be all right. That’s why I am smiling with a sigh of relief”. I had to undergo angioplasty on that day. **I started living a healthy life thereafter and believed Health is Wealth**

**Lesson**: Bad times act as warnings which lead to correct your way of life. Health is Wealth   
\------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

**Positive outlook**  
After angioplasty and taking rest for two weeks, I resumed office. Sometimes worries crept into my mind about the chest pain I had. Some relatives, deriving sadistic pleasure, even commented that these stents last for 10 years (Recall my topic on “Sabjantalogy”\!) On reaching the office, I found that preparation of celebration is on with cake cutting ceremony and a “healthy” lunch”. I asked my boss “what is this nonsense about?” He replied “Look Abhik, you had blockage and have undergone angioplasty. In fact you were lucky to have chest pain and land in the hospital. Now with the stents implanted, the blood is flowing merrily through your arteries. And your life is extended to some decades. But we live anxiously with blockages, as all humans have, and we do not know what fate lies upon us”. I really wondered about his positive thought. It gave me hope and strength to live on happily leaving behind my worries.

**Lesson**: Live happily with hope; hope provides strength at all times  
\------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

**Trust, Hope, Faith and Confidence**  
I want to share a series of very short stories which made an impact on my life. The first one – you may have observed that a father plays with his small child by throwing him up on the air and again holding him tightly on his fall. As the child is thrown, he giggles with laughter and joy but has no fear of falling. Why? Because he knows for certain that his father is there to hold him for sure while he falls. **This is TRUST**. The second one \- Once on a hot summer afternoon, the village head asked all the villagers to gather at 10 am the next day and pray to rain god to give rains to the palace. Next day all the people, young and old, came under the scorching heat to pray to the rain god. A little young girl came with an umbrella over her head. When asked, she said it will rain after our prayers. **This is HOPE.** The third one – Sometimes when a cricketer goes to a field to bat, he looks up at the sun. Or someone prays to God before he goes to take an examination. I have also seen footballers touching the field with respect before he starts playing for a match. What are all these gestures like looking up at the sun or praying to God or touching the football field? The individuals believe that they will succeed if they resort to such acts. **This is FAITH**. The fourth one – A student studies very well for the IIT exams. He solves all the past papers. He takes coaching classes and scores very well in all the mock tests. He says he is sure to crack the exams. **This is CONFIDENCE**. 

**Lesson**: Live and work with Trust, Hope, Faith and Confidence and implement your Knowledge and Skills, you are sure to succeed  
\------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

**Be perseverant**  
When I became the Marketing Head in charge of Business Development, Marketing and Bidding in 2007, I started submitting tenders to various clients. As months passed by, I found that I was losing all the tenders and it’s a tough job. Almost 8 months passed and I lost almost 20 tenders successively. Not a single one I could win. Then one day I was on a tour and I heard that a meeting of the entire office was held by the then Business Head. On my return, I got a call from my colleague and I learnt that I was severely criticized as a Head and for my failures in the last 8 months. Not only this, The Business Head proposed to replace my position with someone better. I was very sad. I did not speak a single word about this in the office. But I wondered what wrong I am doing all these days. In the next tender, I went through the nitty-gritties of the costing, worked out every minute detail with my colleagues and submitted our price bid. The project was big and prestigious. Eventually we became L1 and were awarded the project. My Business Head changed his mind and congratulated me. I still remember my friend saying to me “Abhik, there is light at the end of each tunnel; Never lose hope”. There was no looking back again. Tenders after tenders were won and I became a prominent figure in the industry, recognized and appreciated with jealousy by our competitors too.

In another instance, for some reason or the other, I was not getting promotion for many years though I was doing good work. Every year after the appraisals, my mother used to ask me “Did you get promotion this time”. “No” was my answer. This answer went on for consecutive years. I still worked hard. I learnt to be perseverant. I consoled myself by understanding that there may not have been enough vacancies for promotion or that some were better than me. Nothing prevented me to carry on with my hard work. I always focused on excelling on every job.  

**Lesson**: Never lose hope; Try and try; Be perseverant; Focus on your work: You are bound to succeed  
\------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ 

**Humble & Caring**  
I heard from my seniors that once in Bombay House, the corporate office of the Tata group, a new liftman was employed. In the very first day of office of the liftman armed with all the rules of using the lift, he turned away a tall gentleman who wanted to use the lift to go to first floor. The rules did not allow this and the gentleman walked up the stairs. All his floe colleagues rushed to him and said “Hey\! Are you mad? Why did you make him go up the stairs? Don’t you know who he is?” The liftman was amazed and said “No, I just followed the rules”. One of the staff then said “You are surely going to lose your job. The gentleman was Sir Ratan Tata, our Chairman”. The liftman almost lost his sense in shock. He was cursing himself. He knew he will be called sometime later and be sacked. He was very sad to have done such a mistake without knowing though he followed the rules. And after sometime, someone came down and conveyed to the liftman that the Chairman have called him. By this time the liftman was half dead. He sobbingly went upstairs and found the Chairman with his entire team assembled in the middle of the hall. He thought he was in the midst of a punishment in public. The Chairman started saying “look gentlemen; here is a man I found doing his duties very obediently and managing people on the lift irrespective of who you are. He sent me walking to this floor and did not allow me to take the lift. I appreciate his duties because he was following all the rules of using the lift”. This very same day the liftman was rewarded. Such was the greatness of Sir Ratan Tata.

In another instance, I was watching a TV interview with the staff of Infosys. The staff was mentioning that the Chairman, Mr Narayan Murthy sometimes visits the residential block where labour resides and enquires the wellbeing of the people, whether street lights, drinking water and other amenities are in place. Such feelings for the labour staff are highly appreciated of a great person.

In another instance of our President, Dr Abdul Kalam, who was also the Chairman of DRDO, asked one of his colleagues, who was a work alcoholic, to take his wife and children to a movie in the evening after office. The person was so busy with his work that he realized its already night and repented that he forgot to take his family to the movie. He left office and reached home with guilt on his face. He apologized to his wife for his forgetfulness. But the wife said that they had already seen the movie and come. He was amazed. He exclaimed “How”. The wife said “since you were busy in work and it was time for the movie, Dr Abdul Kalam came to our house himself and took us for the movie”. Can someone imagine the greatness of this gentleman\!

The moral of these stories is that treat all people, even your juniors, with kindness and consideration. You do not lose your ego by being kind and considerate to your juniors, rather you demonstrate yourself as a great leader and a human being.

**Lesson**: Be humble; Be caring; That is what a great leader is.  
\------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

**Appreciate Performers**  
Once I started doing well as the Marketing Head winning sizeable number of bids, my MD wished that the top 10 performers of the company, which included me too, be facilitated by the then Chairman of the Tata Group, Mr Cyrus Mistry. It was a moment in my corporate life to be cherished forever. Mr Mistry came to our HO at Hyderabad and facilitated me and the others with a sincere word of appreciation. I cannot forget ever the gesture of my MD and the Chairman to have done this for me. I remain ever grateful to them. And later in life, I cried when I heard the news of Mr Mistry’s death.

In another instance, when I was already succeeding as Marketing Head winning bids, my MD came to our office for a meeting with my bosses. The bosses accompanied the MD upstairs to our office and headed for his cabin past my own. When he saw me, he first came inside my cabin along with my bosses and almost fell on my feet.  I was shocked. I said “No No sir, please do not do that”. He said “Abhik, winning bids only allow our company to execute projects and everyone has a job to do in this company only when you win bids, so I am grateful to you and your team”. Tears rolled down my years on seeing his appreciation. I had no words to say.

**Lesson**: Appreciate performers; Encourage and Reward them  
\------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

**Silence**  
Sometimes when we argue and protest for something, the other person may be adamant, not willing to listen to your logic, and keep on arguing to prove that you are wrong. It happens in the corporate too. Particularly when you argue with your seniors like a senior relative or your boss to put your points in place and the other person does not agree and keep on arguing, you find yourself frustrated wasting unnecessary time. In such times, I have stayed away from such people with no contacts at all for long durations. In the corporate, you can maintain silence to the extent possible except for when you need to discuss or interact. Often I have observed that your silence speaks much louder than words and helps the other person to realize that he was adamant and wrong. I have adopted this policy even with my relatives whenever required.

**Lesson**: Silence is often the best form of protest  
\------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

**Be Respectful**  
Once my mother rebuked me for no wrong I had done. I gave her back. I did not understand the pain I inflicted on her. I loved her very much and I repented that I was harsh and aggressive. I could have replied in a much respectful manner and not insult her, however, sticking to my point of view.

In a similar manner, I had scolded a colleague very harshly who was much junior in position to me but much senior to me by age. I saw him almost on the verge of collapsing. I learnt one big lesson in life. Even if you have to reprimand someone, particularly higher in age, for something, you can do it in a respectful manner without being harsh. If required, both can share their point of view for better understanding. Even strict punishments can be given as and when necessary, but it has to be in a respectful manner and humanly because older people probably cannot digest insult as easily as they could when they were young. Be polite and respectful to senior people.  That is a sign of good leadership.

**Lesson**: Be respectful; It is a sign of good leadership  
\------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

**Whatever happens, it happens for good**  
Look at so many incidences I narrated before. I met with an accident while coming home from a seminar, but the incident revealed who my true friends were. I was hospitalized after a severe chest pain and underwent angioplasty, but the event taught me to live healthier and realize that Health is Wealth. I did not get promotion for long eight years in my early part of my career, but it helped me to be more perseverant and live with Trust, Hope, Faith and Confidence. When great leaders like the former Tata group Chairman and my MD facilitated me for my success, I learnt what appreciation by humble and caring leaders mean. When seniors accused me wrongly, I learnt how silence can be the best form of protest.

Even when my father and mother died, I thought that it is for their good that their souls would be relieved from the sufferings and pain they were in. It pained me a lot on their demise, but I believed that the truth of death being certain is a creation of the Almighty for something good. Maybe that helped me to become independent from the shelter of my parents and be able to do tough work for which I was dependent on my parents. Loss of your loved one probably provides you greater strength to live on with your life, though at times the emotions of the loss is immeasurable.

However, I would like to introduce some new parameters which I have not seen any MIS, yet I feel they are important indications to the health of a project. Let us discus one by one.

But if the actual time spent from the date of award is 7 months, then your project is 2 months (9-2) ahead of schedule.

If the money earned % is far less than the time lapsed %, it means that the activities have not been quoted appropriately.
  3933→
  3934→Also intelligent bidders tend to front load the activities, which mean quoting more prices for activities to be done in the beginning and quoting lesser for activities to be done later. In this manner, you can better the money earned % with respect to time lapsed % meaning you earn more with respect to time. This leads the project to a better cash flow. file.content:
**SECTION 5 – ARTICLES**

**PART 27**

**NEW CONCEPTS**

Innovation is the mother of invention. When Edison’s trial for lighting the bulb failed a hundred of times, people laughed. When Einstein discovered the gravitational theory, people laughed. People often laugh at new things thinking they are a joke. But eventually all such innovations become reality. 

Therefore, try to innovate and find new ways to do things which are smarter, easier and shorter. Sometimes when you adopt new practices, people may not like to adopt or some may not like them because of sheer jealousy, but eventually people will adopt when they find them better in due course of time. You have to give time and explain about its benefits compared to earlier practices. There is a very good saying in English – “the only constant today is change”. So, change, change and change but change for the better so that employees would welcome and adopt such changes.

I have picked up certain issues in the following chapters which can be subjected to introspection by you. You may like some, and not like some. But they give you some food for thought.

**CHAPTER : PROJECT HEALTH CARD**

Have you seen a job card maintained by the car maintenance engineer when you take a car to a workshop? Have you seen your own health card comprising results of your blood tests and other tests performed by the diagnostic laboratory?

Similarly why not a Project Health card? Many would say what is new about this? MIS on Projects display various project data for managers to take necessary action. That’s 100% true. However, I would like to introduce some new parameters which I have not seen any MIS, yet I feel they are important indications to the health of a project. Let us discus one by one.

**Time lapsed**   
Suppose a high voltage transmission line project involves casting of 500 foundations, erecting of 500 tower structures and stringing (ie. Installing of overhead wires on top of the erected towers) of 499 spans between towers. Depending on physical time required, I put a weightage of 40% on foundations, 35% on tower erection and balance 25% on stringing.

At any point of time, suppose 200 foundations are cast, 150 towers erected and 100 spans strung. Let us calculate the time lapsed.

Time lapsed \= (0.4 \* 200/500) \+ (0.35 \* 150/500) \+ (0.25 \* 100/499)  
		\= 0.16 \+ 0.105 \+ 0.126  
		\= 0.391, ie. 39.10% of time taken 

Suppose the completion period for the above project was 24 months.  
Therefore time lapsed \= 0.391 \* 24 \=  9.384 months, say 9 months for simplicity

Now, if the actual time spent from the date of award is 12 months to complete those 200 foundations, 150 towers. 100 spans of stringing, then your project is delayed by 3 months (12-9) because you have actually done a 9 month progress in a physical 12 month spent. But if the actual time spent from the date of award is 7 months, then your project is 2 months (9-2) ahead of schedule.

The above example is a simple exercise with 3 activities only. You may list down more number of major activities (major meaning taking significant time) and do the exercise from time to time. This is a great way to determine the progress of a project broadly.

**Money earned**  
Every activity is associated with billing. Say foundations comprise of 25% of total price, tower erections 45% of total price and stringing balance 30% on total price.

Taking the same example as above,  
Money earned \= (0.25 \* 200/500) \+ (0.45 \* 150/500) \+ (0.30 \* 100/499)  
     \= 0.10 \+ 0.135 \+ 0.06  
                            \= 0.295, ie. 29.5% of total price 

Therefore in 39.10% of time consumed, I have earned 29.50% of total contract price. In other words, I have spent more percentage of time than I have earned as a percentage of total contract price.

It is obvious that the above two percentages cannot be same, however they should not differ much because the hardships and time consumed in executing an activity should be correlated to the price quoted for the activity. If the money earned % is far less than the time lapsed %, it means that the activities have not been quoted appropriately.

Also intelligent bidders tend to front load the activities, which mean quoting more prices for activities to be done in the beginning and quoting lesser for activities to be done later. In this manner, you can better the money earned % with respect to time lapsed % meaning you earn more with respect to time. This leads the project to a better cash flow. However, there is a limit to which front loadings can be done otherwise the unit prices of the activities may seem to be unreasonable.

**CHAPTER : THE PRODUCTIVITY MEASUREMENT**

1119→**CHAPTER : THE “FOREIGN TOUR” SYNDROME**
  1120→
  1121→In many organizations, there is a craze amongst senior eligible employees to go abroad for official work. While such official works are necessary for the growth of an organization, their other interests are also to save some time and visit tourist spots and also save (earn) money from the daily dollar/euro allowance that he gets for each day. 
  1122→
  1123→There is no harm in touring abroad for business opportunities or for preparation of future bids. But such visits cannot go on and on at the cost of company’s expenses without any significant results in some period of time. I agree that there is a gestation period for any new business to take off; you simply cannot expect a business to come from a new market after one visit to the country, but at the same time there is always a limit for that. You cannot go abroad dozens of times and yet significant results don’t come.
  1124→
  1125→I have come across such senior people who have gone to the same country again and again to seek opportunity in a new domain of business, even with the support of his Director, but without any significant progress even after multiple visits. You can of course go multiple times to a same country but the progress of each visit must be appreciable. A company spends lot of money for a foreign travel. You cannot take a company to task by going several times and yet not produce any appreciable progress. 
  1126→
  1127→The best method to keep a track of the progress of foreign visits is to maintain a register noting the travel details, duration, money spent, meetings held and progress till date. Minutes of the Tour (MoT) detailing discussions held in the meetings and forthcoming actions needs to be recorded. The register should also set a budget for the marketing expenses and the cost of visits to foreign countries and the expectation of returns in terms of obtaining business. If these expenses are very high with respect to benefits achieved or benefits estimated to receive in future, then such marketing in the country under question may be abandoned. 
  1128→
  1129→A cost-returns matrix must be made to analyse probabilities of obtaining business. Returns must be multiple times of the cost. While getting business, the revenues cannot be considered as returns, rather profits earned from the business should be considered as returns because profits are the actual amounts earned by a company, not the revenues. The returns may be low in the beginning but with each passing year the returns must be more before the business gets saturated at a future point of time.  
  1130→For example, assume that the cost of visits, marketing and setting up an office in a foreign country is Rs 3 Crores in a year. A leader, say, spends two years into marketing in the country before he starts getting a business. After two years, say, the organization gets two orders of Rs 100 Cr each, both with a completion period of 2 years. Assume that each project is completed by 50% each year and the projects make a margin of 5%. Then here is the Marketing cost-returns matrix (All figures are in Rs Cr)
  1131→
  1132→Year	Opening	Cost		Orders  	Executed 	Profit		Closing  
  1133→	Balance	incurred	Received	Revenues	Margin	Balance  
  1134→1	0		3		0		0		0		0  
  1135→2	0		3		0		0		0		0  
  1136→3	0		3		200		100		5		100  
  1137→4	100		3		200		150		7.5		150  
  1138→5	150		3		200		175		8.75		175  
  1139→6	175		3		200		187.5		9.375		187.5  
  1140→Total after 6 years:	18		800		612.5		30.625
  1141→
  1142→Above was a simple exercise. I have not considered the time value of money to make it simple to understand. You can observe that after 6 years, while you have incurred a cumulative cost of Rs 18 Cr, you have earned profits of Rs 30.625 Cr. But for this, you need a continuous flow of orders and a continuous executing to earn the profits.  
  1143→    
  1144→Future business is all about estimates from your experience. You have to be rational in your expectations considering the business potential available in the country, the competition existing, the strategy you employ to secure business, etc. If your expectations were as above, it makes sense to continue marketing efforts for the next couple of years as you can find from the Cost-Returns matrix though the gestation period in the above example was 2 years, in other words, there was no earnings in the first two years but the expenses had to be incurred.  
  1145→
  1146→**The salient points are:**
  1147→
  1148→1. Evaluate MoTs after every foreign tour to determine progress made  
  1149→2. Abandon or continue foreign tours based on progressive results and future estimated returns  
  1150→3. Determine Marketing-cost matrix to understand worthwhileness of your efforts
  1151→
  1152→**THE “FORCE YOUR STYLE” SYNDROME**
  1153→
  1154→Leaders, irrespective of whether you are the MD or a Director or any CXO or a SBU Head or a BU Head or a Departmental Head, have different styles of leadership patterns. These patterns are generally developed by individual leaders while they were growing up the ladder to senior positions and eventually became top leaders.
  1155→
  1156→All leadership styles are different from each other, some may be effective for some, and some others may be effective for others. There are nothing called right or wrong kinds of leadership. All leadership styles have advantages and disadvantages and that is why some leaders tactfully employ different leadership styles at different times.
  1157→
  1158→Once there was a CXO who always insisted his style of work to his subordinate. The CXO thought that his kind of leadership style is the only effective style that everyone needs to adopt. The CXO did not appreciate the subordinate having a different style of effective leadership and did not think that forcing upon him another kind of leadership style will not help him to achieve desired results. A top leader must be interested in achieving the result irrespective of the leadership style that his subordinate follows so long the leadership style is effective, ethical and SMART. Bulldozing a leader’s style down below is a sign of being adamant and displaying dictatorship.
  1159→
  1160→One must develop his own style of leadership and the style which his boss or his leader adopts may not necessarily be copied. The leader must also be democratic allowing his subordinate to follow his own style of leadership so long the results are ethically effective. 
  1161→
  1162→I have not discussed here the different style of leadership in practice which you can get to read in any leadership book. This chapter is on how you expect others to practice leadership. Interestingly, long time back, I had come across an article in a newspaper narrating the Gandhian style and the Hitlarian style of leadership. I was impressed by its different styles and I used to follow in the corporate having found it very effective. These styles have been described in an Annexure later.

  1165→
  1166→1. Develop your own style of leadership as you feel right. Don’t copy styles of your leaders unnecessarily  
  1167→2. You may adopt different styles to be effective in different circumstances
  1168→

  1170→
  1171→Once, one BU Head, who was reporting to his SBU Head, was given the responsibility of securing a negotiated project in Africa. The award of project required a competitive pricing as well as lot of documentation to prove the credibility and experience of the organization. 
  1172→
  1173→Meetings were held occasionally and documents had to be revised or modified in line with the client’s requirements from time to time. Sometimes there were new documents required to be submitted. The SBU Head was to retire in a few months from then. The SBU Head, during review meetings, enquired regarding the progress of the expected award and found that the progress was quite slow.
  1174→
  1175→Actually, the BU Head was processing the case slowly. He was not in a mood to speed up the award process, in which case the SBU Head would take the major credit for receiving the award before his retirement was due. The objective of the BU Head was to go slow in such a manner that the award would be received after the retirement of the SBU Head, in which case he would have the entire liberty to claim full credit for bagging the award.
  1176→
  1177→The above attitude is condemnable. Firstly, the SBU Head also had a role to play to receive the award based on his initial negotiations. The BU Head trying to discredit him by receiving the award intentionally after the SBU Head’s retirement is not right in its first place. Secondly, life teaches that opportunity does not come again and again. In case you intentionally delay to receive an award, you risk your organization by giving your competitor to sneak in and grab the opportunity in which case you may not be considered for the award. Thirdly, every organization has quarterly and annual targets to be achieved. By delaying receipt of awards, you are actually depriving the organization to achieve the quarterly or annual targets. Even if the annual targets were in place, the award received would have increased the annual orders leading to better increments and bonus to the employees.
  1178→
  1179→Therefore the SBU Head must be vigilant if any of his subordinates are intentionally delaying any process in the organization. For this, regular reviews should be in place and the problem of delay identified right at the beginning. If required, the responsibility can be shifted to some other honest individual who would try his best to speed up the process of an expected award. Deliberate procrastination must be dealt with severely.  
  1180→
  1181→**The salient points are:**
  1182→
  1183→1. Deliberate procrastination is highly condemned. It may rob the organization of opportunities  
  1184→2. Intentional procrastination to credit oneself and discredit someone else who also deserves credit is a crime. Such leaders must be dealt with sternly

  1189→
  1190→**Skip level meetings**

In fact sometimes the reporting level tries to suppress the voices of their subordinates to their bosses for vested reasons. In a skip level meeting with a leader, the leader keeps his reporting level in abeyance and interacts with the employees’ one level below so that the leader is aware of important things that may have been ignored or kept secret by his reporting level. Understanding the business processes may be clearer during interaction in the skip level meetings. There could be good suggestions too coming up during these discussions which the reporting level might have ignored.
  1192→
  1193→**Democratic leadership**

  1195→
  1196→**Supportive delegation**

The clients and vendors can be immediately informed about critical issues that the organization is facing. Different Heads can be roped in as required to boost the performance in projects. Each project should be reviewed in details including its financials and action points noted. The overheads may also be determined to keep a track of the same. Market share analysis must also be tracked every month including those of the competitors.

There could be areas concerning software requirements, or modifying existing processes, or boosting the hardware of the existing IT systems, or restructuring ergonomics in the office space, etc. Solving work related issues leads to better job satisfaction amongst employees, optimizes time and provides a “feel good” factor, all leading to a better work culture in the organization.

  1212→Good monthly performance should be rewarded. This will create a healthy competition amongst employees in the future which will improve organizational performance as a whole. Also the morale of the winners will be boosted for better performance. Non winners can be more enthusiastic to try harder to get an award in the next month.
  1213→
  1214→**Unconventional thinking**  
  1215→Support and appreciate unconventional thinking. Many new thoughts may not be found to be effective, but some may be exceptionally brilliant. Arouse the culture of unconventional thinking in the organisation. May be you have a quarterly session for the purpose. 
  1216→
  1217→**Find the right successor**  
  1218→Depending on potential, you have to pick someone and train him as your successor. Leaders must ensure the future of an organization and hence its importance.
  1219→
  1220→**Promote knowledge and skills to employees through Training**  
  1221→There is no end to learning in our lives. From a newly born baby till death, we learn so many things in our lives on a daily basis. Coming to corporates, similarly, training in different fields of expertise must be carried on to enhance the capabilities of employees. This will help employees improve and achieve desired goals of the organization.
  1222→
  1223→**Meeting each Department quarterly**  
  1224→Each supporting Department has issues of their own. All such issues cannot be looked into in a monthly MIS meeting which is basically driven to review business results. Yet Departmental issues play a critical role in the performance of an organization. Hence looking into issues faced by each Department, at least once in a quarter, and resolving them is of significant importance.
  1225→
  1226→**Be unbiased towards all employees**  
  1227→Treat all equally based on performance and potential, irrespective of sex, caste, creed, colour, religion and also importantly irrespective of your personal relationship. This will create a healthy environment in the organization. Employees will believe what is being said and done is right. It will create trust amongst employees and also towards the organization and foster healthy environment. If you are treated equally, there will be a tendency to accept if you are wrong at times. It builds up an atmosphere of true spirit in the organization. 
  1228→
  1229→**Communicate well**  
  1230→Once every month, send a communication across the organization on all the developments that happened in the month and the future endeavours discussed. Employees will take pride to work in the organization knowing well about the developments and likely future. Proper communication will also arrest rumours and grape vine, if any, floating in and around the organization.  
  1231→

  1233→Ask HR team to send your wishes for birthday of your employees. Birthday wishes brings happiness to employees, that too from senior leaders. This creates togetherness in an organization. Employees feel proud that their leaders remember their days of joy
  1234→
  1235→**Get Well Soon cards**  
  1236→Ask HR to send Get Well Soon cards to employees suffering from an ailment and on leave. Employees would feel that they are cared for. They will always give their best performance to their leaders and the organization once they resume work.

  1239→

  1242→**PEOPLE, THE EMPLOYEES**
  1243→

It’s the people who make the name and fame of an organisation; it’s the people who execute the business to achieve its objectives and build reputation for the organization.

For example, an organisation today, even if it’s size is small, has multiple sources to obtain finance, be it banks or other financial institutions, at low interest rates. With such finance, it can purchase good quality equipment and other infrastructure, software and hardware to conduct its business. **CHAPTER : THE “FOREIGN TOUR” SYNDROME**

In many organizations, there is a craze amongst senior eligible employees to go abroad for official work. While such official works are necessary for the growth of an organization, their other interests are also to save some time and visit tourist spots and also save (earn) money from the daily dollar/euro allowance that he gets for each day. 

There is no harm in touring abroad for business opportunities or for preparation of future bids. But such visits cannot go on and on at the cost of company’s expenses without any significant results in some period of time. I agree that there is a gestation period for any new business to take off; you simply cannot expect a business to come from a new market after one visit to the country, but at the same time there is always a limit for that. You cannot go abroad dozens of times and yet significant results don’t come.

I have come across such senior people who have gone to the same country again and again to seek opportunity in a new domain of business, even with the support of his Director, but without any significant progress even after multiple visits. You can of course go multiple times to a same country but the progress of each visit must be appreciable. A company spends lot of money for a foreign travel. You cannot take a company to task by going several times and yet not produce any appreciable progress. 

The best method to keep a track of the progress of foreign visits is to maintain a register noting the travel details, duration, money spent, meetings held and progress till date. Minutes of the Tour (MoT) detailing discussions held in the meetings and forthcoming actions needs to be recorded. The register should also set a budget for the marketing expenses and the cost of visits to foreign countries and the expectation of returns in terms of obtaining business. If these expenses are very high with respect to benefits achieved or benefits estimated to receive in future, then such marketing in the country under question may be abandoned. 

A cost-returns matrix must be made to analyse probabilities of obtaining business. Returns must be multiple times of the cost. While getting business, the revenues cannot be considered as returns, rather profits earned from the business should be considered as returns because profits are the actual amounts earned by a company, not the revenues. The returns may be low in the beginning but with each passing year the returns must be more before the business gets saturated at a future point of time.  
For example, assume that the cost of visits, marketing and setting up an office in a foreign country is Rs 3 Crores in a year. A leader, say, spends two years into marketing in the country before he starts getting a business. After two years, say, the organization gets two orders of Rs 100 Cr each, both with a completion period of 2 years. Assume that each project is completed by 50% each year and the projects make a margin of 5%. Then here is the Marketing cost-returns matrix (All figures are in Rs Cr)

Year	Opening	Cost		Orders  	Executed 	Profit		Closing  
	Balance	incurred	Received	Revenues	Margin	Balance  
1	0		3		0		0		0		0  
2	0		3		0		0		0		0  
3	0		3		200		100		5		100  
4	100		3		200		150		7.5		150  
5	150		3		200		175		8.75		175  
6	175		3		200		187.5		9.375		187.5  
Total after 6 years:	18		800		612.5		30.625

Above was a simple exercise. I have not considered the time value of money to make it simple to understand. You can observe that after 6 years, while you have incurred a cumulative cost of Rs 18 Cr, you have earned profits of Rs 30.625 Cr. But for this, you need a continuous flow of orders and a continuous executing to earn the profits.  
    
Future business is all about estimates from your experience. You have to be rational in your expectations considering the business potential available in the country, the competition existing, the strategy you employ to secure business, etc. If your expectations were as above, it makes sense to continue marketing efforts for the next couple of years as you can find from the Cost-Returns matrix though the gestation period in the above example was 2 years, in other words, there was no earnings in the first two years but the expenses had to be incurred.  

**The salient points are:**

1. Evaluate MoTs after every foreign tour to determine progress made  
2. Abandon or continue foreign tours based on progressive results and future estimated returns  
3. Determine Marketing-cost matrix to understand worthwhileness of your efforts

**THE “FORCE YOUR STYLE” SYNDROME**

Leaders, irrespective of whether you are the MD or a Director or any CXO or a SBU Head or a BU Head or a Departmental Head, have different styles of leadership patterns. These patterns are generally developed by individual leaders while they were growing up the ladder to senior positions and eventually became top leaders.

All leadership styles are different from each other, some may be effective for some, and some others may be effective for others. There are nothing called right or wrong kinds of leadership. All leadership styles have advantages and disadvantages and that is why some leaders tactfully employ different leadership styles at different times.

Once there was a CXO who always insisted his style of work to his subordinate. The CXO thought that his kind of leadership style is the only effective style that everyone needs to adopt. The CXO did not appreciate the subordinate having a different style of effective leadership and did not think that forcing upon him another kind of leadership style will not help him to achieve desired results. A top leader must be interested in achieving the result irrespective of the leadership style that his subordinate follows so long the leadership style is effective, ethical and SMART. Bulldozing a leader’s style down below is a sign of being adamant and displaying dictatorship.

One must develop his own style of leadership and the style which his boss or his leader adopts may not necessarily be copied. The leader must also be democratic allowing his subordinate to follow his own style of leadership so long the results are ethically effective. 

I have not discussed here the different style of leadership in practice which you can get to read in any leadership book. This chapter is on how you expect others to practice leadership. Interestingly, long time back, I had come across an article in a newspaper narrating the Gandhian style and the Hitlarian style of leadership. I was impressed by its different styles and I used to follow in the corporate having found it very effective. These styles have been described in an Annexure later.  
   
**The salient points are:**

1. Develop your own style of leadership as you feel right. Don’t copy styles of your leaders unnecessarily  
2. You may adopt different styles to be effective in different circumstances

**THE “DELIBERATE PROCASTINATION” SYNDROME**

Once, one BU Head, who was reporting to his SBU Head, was given the responsibility of securing a negotiated project in Africa. The award of project required a competitive pricing as well as lot of documentation to prove the credibility and experience of the organization. 

Meetings were held occasionally and documents had to be revised or modified in line with the client’s requirements from time to time. Sometimes there were new documents required to be submitted. The SBU Head was to retire in a few months from then. The SBU Head, during review meetings, enquired regarding the progress of the expected award and found that the progress was quite slow.

Actually, the BU Head was processing the case slowly. He was not in a mood to speed up the award process, in which case the SBU Head would take the major credit for receiving the award before his retirement was due. The objective of the BU Head was to go slow in such a manner that the award would be received after the retirement of the SBU Head, in which case he would have the entire liberty to claim full credit for bagging the award.

The above attitude is condemnable. Firstly, the SBU Head also had a role to play to receive the award based on his initial negotiations. The BU Head trying to discredit him by receiving the award intentionally after the SBU Head’s retirement is not right in its first place. Secondly, life teaches that opportunity does not come again and again. In case you intentionally delay to receive an award, you risk your organization by giving your competitor to sneak in and grab the opportunity in which case you may not be considered for the award. Thirdly, every organization has quarterly and annual targets to be achieved. By delaying receipt of awards, you are actually depriving the organization to achieve the quarterly or annual targets. Even if the annual targets were in place, the award received would have increased the annual orders leading to better increments and bonus to the employees.

Therefore the SBU Head must be vigilant if any of his subordinates are intentionally delaying any process in the organization. For this, regular reviews should be in place and the problem of delay identified right at the beginning. If required, the responsibility can be shifted to some other honest individual who would try his best to speed up the process of an expected award. Deliberate procrastination must be dealt with severely.  

**The salient points are:**

1. Deliberate procrastination is highly condemned. It may rob the organization of opportunities  
2. Intentional procrastination to credit oneself and discredit someone else who also deserves credit is a crime. Such leaders must be dealt with sternly  
3. Regular reviews must be held to arrest deliberate procrastination. If required, the responsibility can be shifted to others  
   

**SOME GOOD PRACTICES**

**Skip level meetings**  
Often the level below yours do not get chance to voice their views to your bosses. In fact sometimes the reporting level tries to suppress the voices of their subordinates to their bosses for vested reasons. In a skip level meeting with a leader, the leader keeps his reporting level in abeyance and interacts with the employees’ one level below so that the leader is aware of important things that may have been ignored or kept secret by his reporting level. Understanding the business processes may be clearer during interaction in the skip level meetings. There could be good suggestions too coming up during these discussions which the reporting level might have ignored.

**Democratic leadership**  
In major decisions, take inputs from your team members. After all it is the team members who have to deliver. By considering their inputs, you raise their morale, motivation, level of engagement and confidence. Do not be autocratic for major decisions. 

**Supportive delegation**  
When you delegate jobs to people, make sure you provide them with the right support and guidance. Without support, your delegation will be an instruction without results. Proper support engages people well avoiding confusion and frustration.

**Team building**  
When you build a team with members having diverse capabilities, you succeed in your ventures. Individual brilliance seldom achieves corporate goals. Developing team creates a supportive work environment.

**Monthly MIS review**  
This will track progress in all aspects of business. It will give insights to areas of improvement. The problem areas can be identified and addressed during such meetings. The clients and vendors can be immediately informed about critical issues that the organization is facing. Different Heads can be roped in as required to boost the performance in projects. Each project should be reviewed in details including its financials and action points noted. The overheads may also be determined to keep a track of the same. Market share analysis must also be tracked every month including those of the competitors.

**Quarterly revision of targets**  
If need be, based on unknown factors earlier and the market conditions, the KRAs may be tweaked to make them reasonable and SMART. Goals which are SMART are accepted by employees with a challenge.  

**Monthly meetings for work environment and organizational issues**  
Apart from business results understood from MIS reviews, work related issues are identified in these meetings. There could be areas concerning software requirements, or modifying existing processes, or boosting the hardware of the existing IT systems, or restructuring ergonomics in the office space, etc. Solving work related issues leads to better job satisfaction amongst employees, optimizes time and provides a “feel good” factor, all leading to a better work culture in the organization.

**Monthly recognition Certificates to the best performer**  
Good monthly performance should be rewarded. This will create a healthy competition amongst employees in the future which will improve organizational performance as a whole. Also the morale of the winners will be boosted for better performance. Non winners can be more enthusiastic to try harder to get an award in the next month.

**Unconventional thinking**  
Support and appreciate unconventional thinking. Many new thoughts may not be found to be effective, but some may be exceptionally brilliant. Arouse the culture of unconventional thinking in the organisation. May be you have a quarterly session for the purpose. 

**Find the right successor**  
Depending on potential, you have to pick someone and train him as your successor. Leaders must ensure the future of an organization and hence its importance.

**Promote knowledge and skills to employees through Training**  
There is no end to learning in our lives. From a newly born baby till death, we learn so many things in our lives on a daily basis. Coming to corporates, similarly, training in different fields of expertise must be carried on to enhance the capabilities of employees. This will help employees improve and achieve desired goals of the organization.

**Meeting each Department quarterly**  
Each supporting Department has issues of their own. All such issues cannot be looked into in a monthly MIS meeting which is basically driven to review business results. Yet Departmental issues play a critical role in the performance of an organization. Hence looking into issues faced by each Department, at least once in a quarter, and resolving them is of significant importance.

**Be unbiased towards all employees**  
Treat all equally based on performance and potential, irrespective of sex, caste, creed, colour, religion and also importantly irrespective of your personal relationship. This will create a healthy environment in the organization. Employees will believe what is being said and done is right. It will create trust amongst employees and also towards the organization and foster healthy environment. If you are treated equally, there will be a tendency to accept if you are wrong at times. It builds up an atmosphere of true spirit in the organization. 

**Communicate well**  
Once every month, send a communication across the organization on all the developments that happened in the month and the future endeavours discussed. Employees will take pride to work in the organization knowing well about the developments and likely future. Proper communication will also arrest rumours and grape vine, if any, floating in and around the organization.  

**Wish on birthdays**  
Ask HR team to send your wishes for birthday of your employees. Birthday wishes brings happiness to employees, that too from senior leaders. This creates togetherness in an organization. Employees feel proud that their leaders remember their days of joy

**Get Well Soon cards**  
Ask HR to send Get Well Soon cards to employees suffering from an ailment and on leave. Employees would feel that they are cared for. They will always give their best performance to their leaders and the organization once they resume work.

**SECTION 2 – PEOPLE**

**PART 2**

**PEOPLE, THE EMPLOYEES**

**The role of people in a corporate**  
As we know and say, people are the most important assets of an organisation. An organisation does not comprise of a name and a huge building made of bricks and mortar. It’s the people who make the name and fame of an organisation; it’s the people who execute the business to achieve its objectives and build reputation for the organization. 

Other assets are also important. But unlike the yesteryears, they are comparatively easier to build. For example, an organisation today, even if it’s size is small, has multiple sources to obtain finance, be it banks or other financial institutions, at low interest rates. With such finance, it can purchase good quality equipment and other infrastructure, software and hardware to conduct its business. With money, you can easily invest good quality assets for your business.

   501→  			**CHAPTER : THE “TOO MANY CXO” SYNDROME**
   502→
   503→Let us see how many CXOs could possibly and theoretically fit in an organisation. The Departments the CXO may head are mentioned within brackets.
   504→
   505→CAO – Chief Administrative Officer (Administration)  
   506→CBO – Chief Business Development Officer (Business Development)  
   507→CCO – Chief Customer Officer (Business Development)  
   508→CDO – Chief Design Officer (Designs & Engineering)  
   509→CEO – Chief Executive Officer (Operations)  
   510→CFO – Chief Finance Officer (Finance)  
   511→CGO – Chief Governance Officer (Audit)  
   512→CHO – Chief Human Resources Officer (HR)  
   513→CIO – Chief Information Technology Officer (Information Technology)  
   514→CJO – Chief of Joint Operations (Operations)  
   515→CKO – Chief Knowledge Officer (Learning & Development)  
   516→CLO – Chief Learning Officer (Learning & Development)  
   517→CMO – Chief Marketing Officer (Marketing)  
   518→CNO – Chief Negotiation Officer (Supply Chain Management)  
   519→COO – Chief Operating Officer (Operations)  
   520→CPO – Chief Procurement Officer (Supply Chain Management)  
   521→CQO – Chief Quality Officer (Quality)  
   522→CRO – Chief Redresser Officer (HR)  
   523→CSO – Chief Strategy Officer (Strategy)  
   524→CTO – Chief Technology Officer (Operations)  
   525→CUO – Chief Unifying Officer (HR)  
   526→CVO – Chief Vigilance Officer (Governance)  
   527→CWO – Chief Wellness Officer (HR)  
   528→CXO – Chief eXperience Officer (Strategy)  
   529→CYO – Chief Youth Officer (Administration)  
   530→CZO – Chief Zone Officer (Operations)
   531→
   532→Some names sound funny, isn’t it? That was just to have fun and complete the series from A to Z. You can also name them as you wish\! Now let us come to the main point. 
   533→
   534→Sometimes it is observed that the Top Leader tends to recruit several CXOs thinking that it will help in different activities of the organisation seamlessly and the Top Leader can be relatively free from interventions in all the activities of the Departments. However, the problems of recruiting many CXOs are as follows:
   535→
   536→1\. The Top becomes heavy and the Employee Overheads increases, as a result of which the company becomes less competitive in the market. The Top Management must be the lightest economically in an organisation. Their total salaries and number in an organisation must be restricted to avoid a Top Heavy Organisation. Their individual salaries may be high but, just to provide an idea, for a large organization, their total salaries could be around 10% of the company’s Cost to Company (CTC) and numbers restricted to around 1% of company’s total employee strength. 
   537→
   538→The Middle Level is kept heavier and the Junior Level is kept the heaviest, both in terms of total salary and numbers. Junior Level is the implementing level, hence the heaviest. However, I have observed too many CXOs being recruited by some companies making them top heavy.
   539→
   540→2\. Too many CXOs sometimes provide their own ideas which are contradicting to each other. The leaders below CXOs get confused faced with different directions from the different CXOs. For example, a CFO instructs the international Bidding team not to bid with less than 7% profit margins anywhere abroad, but the CBO asks the same team to bid with Zero profits in a particular country as entry costs to establish as a first time entrant and subsequently increase their presence. The irony is the CFO and CBO may be reluctant to discuss the issue amongst them. 
   541→
   542→3\. Often the CXOs are egoistic and some resort to politics to prove their superiority. They try to create an atmosphere down below that their directions are the most superior and need to be followed even if not suggested by other CXOs. This creates confusion down below as to whose directions need to be followed. In the above example, the CFO might try to impose his directions on the bidding team, rather than take up the matter with the CBO with the intervention of MD and form a consensus.
   543→
   544→4\. Each CXO wants to recruit and strengthen his team in the corporate office though their concerned Departments in various SBUs may have sufficient number of people of similar expertise. For example, the Finance Department of various SBUs may already have a Chartered Accountant (CA), however the CFO may intend to recruit another CA under his belt. Also these recruits, being under CXO and stationed at corporate office, try to flex their muscles to employees of the concerned Departments. This creates a non-cooperation environment between the department and the CXO’s team.
   545→
   546→**“Too many CXO”** becomes a common chant amongst employees when they find one after another CXOs being unnecessarily recruited. 
   547→
   548→The problem is that all departments need a CXO to be monitored and directed. But how many is the question. There are simple solutions to this problem. 
   549→
   550→1. Top Leaders need to combine related functions together to reduce the number of CXO recruits. With lesser number of CXOs, there will be better unity, harmony and clarity of directions in the organisation. For example, Business Development & Marketing, Advertising, Bidding & Costing and Customer Relations can be under the CBO. Finance, Accounts and Audit can be under CFO. Administration, HR, Learning & Development, Wellness department, Unifying Officers, and Governance can be under CHRO. 
   551→
   552→2. Under each CXO, there can be different expertise in his corporate team or in the Departments to look after different activities. For example, under CHRO, there could be different Managers either in his corporate team or in the department to look after Administration, HR, Learning & Development, Wellness, and Unifying activities but CHRO remains as the overall single Chief. Also, under CFO, there could be one Chartered Accountant (CA) in his corporate team and it may not be unnecessarily duplicated by recruiting another CA in the concerned Department.  
   553→     
   554→3. Also, different CXOs should meet together to form a consensus for providing same directions to the Department or SBU Heads. For example, the CSO, CFO and the CBO must align together to decide what profit levels the Bidding team should consider to enter an international market for the first time. The profit margin to be considered could be very less if there is huge competition in that market. It could be negative, in other words, bid with anticipated loss to be sure to win, if the market is very big and the presence and learning of the market during operations is of utmost importance. It could be high if the competition is scarce and past results show that the contractors generally bid with high profit margins. There could be different scenarios, but the essence is the CSO, CBO and the CFO must form a common opinion and guide the Department in one direction. 
   555→
   556→Let me give you another example. Imagine the pandemonium that would be created if the Cricket Control Board thinks to provide best services in international cricket by recruiting a batting coach, a spin bowling coach, a fast bowling coach, a fielding coach, a wicket keeping coach, etc. Instead there can be a single coach who has a broad knowledge of all the areas of the game. In bowling itself, there are different kinds of bowlers like the off spinners, leg spinners, fast bowling, swingers, googly bowlers, etc. but that does not mean that the Board has to recruit different coaches for different kind of bowling. In batting too, some are proficient in hitting sixes, some in cover drives, and some in sweeping. All these batting abilities require different capabilities, however, irrespective of all these variations, the coach needs to be one or maximum at two who can provide all round required expertise to all the players. This would enable to provide all the players a uniform direction.
   557→
   558→Similarly pandemonium will be created if in football there are different coaches like the free kick coach, dribbling coach, penalty shootout coach, goalkeeping coach, mid fielder coach. Isn’t it?
   559→
   560→So, in an organisation, **the CXO strength must be kept to a minimum** and the various related activities of the organisation can be clubbed together under a single CXO, as illustrated above.
   561→
   562→If you become a Managing Director someday, I wish you be so, please carefully study the business model of your organization thoroughly and discuss with key people to layout your structure of CXOs. Modify them if possible if the structure does not suit with your thoughts or align with the business you are in. Provide each CXO clear roles and responsibilities having no conflict amongst each other. Organise meetings between concerned CXOs if they have different views so that a common final view can be cascaded down below to avoid confusion and provide clarity.
   563→
   564→**The salient points are:**
   565→
   566→1. Limit your number of CXO recruits  
   567→2. Limit CXO salaries as a maximum percentage of company salary  
   568→3. Engage relevant CXOs to form a consensus on strategic decisions  
   569→4. Eliminate redundancy in positions amongst Departments & CXO’s office.
   570→
   571→**CHAPTER : THE “RENOWNED GUY” SYNDROME**
   572→
   573→Right person for the right job\! Readers may think what new I am saying. Yes, nothing new. Yet I cannot but state its importance again and again because I have seen organisations faltering in this area.
   574→
   575→I have observed that often very renowned personnel are recruited for important positions but eventually they have been found to be non-performers. Why? Simply because the recruit is not the expertise in the particular field even if he is renowned by name. You have to question what are you actually renowned for?
   576→
   577→For example, with due respect, Sachin Tendulkar was one of the renowned cricket players of the world but he would have probably performed badly if he was made the Hockey captain of India. Similarly Pele or Maradona were renowned footballers on earth, but they were unlikely to have performed as the Cricket captain of Brazil or Argentina. Am I correct? A Gynecologist and a Cardiologist both may have a MBBS degree and may be renowned, but you cannot expect a Gynecologist to perform a Heart Operation. Such blunders do happen in the corporate. Someone in the industry may be very renowned in Marketing but he cannot perform well if he is put into Operations. Sometimes HR gets swayed away on a person being so renowned, but the question is whether he is the right person for the right job that you are looking for?
   578→
   579→Mistakes are committed in another aspect also. Suppose you interview a Procurement officer working in a small sized SBU of a large sized company. The particular SBU may be dealing with few vendors on a small scale of annual procurements. But you get swayed away by the fact that the Procurement officer works in a very large company and thereafter provide him the position of Chief Procurement Officer in your company where you need to deal with hundreds of vendors on a large scale of annual procurements. Can he perform? Definitely not. It’s like asking the pilot of a small propeller jet to fly an A380 Airbus. Imagine the likely fate of the passengers\!

**CHAPTER : THE “TOO MANY CXO” SYNDROME**

Let us see how many CXOs could possibly and theoretically fit in an organisation. The Departments the CXO may head are mentioned within brackets.

CAO – Chief Administrative Officer (Administration)  
CBO – Chief Business Development Officer (Business Development)  
CCO – Chief Customer Officer (Business Development)  
CDO – Chief Design Officer (Designs & Engineering)  
CEO – Chief Executive Officer (Operations)  
CFO – Chief Finance Officer (Finance)  
CGO – Chief Governance Officer (Audit)  
CHO – Chief Human Resources Officer (HR)  
CIO – Chief Information Technology Officer (Information Technology)  
CJO – Chief of Joint Operations (Operations)  
CKO – Chief Knowledge Officer (Learning & Development)  
CLO – Chief Learning Officer (Learning & Development)  
CMO – Chief Marketing Officer (Marketing)  
CNO – Chief Negotiation Officer (Supply Chain Management)  
COO – Chief Operating Officer (Operations)  
CPO – Chief Procurement Officer (Supply Chain Management)  
CQO – Chief Quality Officer (Quality)  
CRO – Chief Redresser Officer (HR)  
CSO – Chief Strategy Officer (Strategy)  
CTO – Chief Technology Officer (Operations)  
CUO – Chief Unifying Officer (HR)  
CVO – Chief Vigilance Officer (Governance)  
CWO – Chief Wellness Officer (HR)  
CXO – Chief eXperience Officer (Strategy)  
CYO – Chief Youth Officer (Administration)  
CZO – Chief Zone Officer (Operations)

Some names sound funny, isn’t it? That was just to have fun and complete the series from A to Z. You can also name them as you wish\! Now let us come to the main point. 

Sometimes it is observed that the Top Leader tends to recruit several CXOs thinking that it will help in different activities of the organisation seamlessly and the Top Leader can be relatively free from interventions in all the activities of the Departments. However, the problems of recruiting many CXOs are as follows:

1\. The Top becomes heavy and the Employee Overheads increases, as a result of which the company becomes less competitive in the market. The Top Management must be the lightest economically in an organisation. Their total salaries and number in an organisation must be restricted to avoid a Top Heavy Organisation. Their individual salaries may be high but, just to provide an idea, for a large organization, their total salaries could be around 10% of the company’s Cost to Company (CTC) and numbers restricted to around 1% of company’s total employee strength. 

The Middle Level is kept heavier and the Junior Level is kept the heaviest, both in terms of total salary and numbers. Junior Level is the implementing level, hence the heaviest. However, I have observed too many CXOs being recruited by some companies making them top heavy.

2\. Too many CXOs sometimes provide their own ideas which are contradicting to each other. The leaders below CXOs get confused faced with different directions from the different CXOs. For example, a CFO instructs the international Bidding team not to bid with less than 7% profit margins anywhere abroad, but the CBO asks the same team to bid with Zero profits in a particular country as entry costs to establish as a first time entrant and subsequently increase their presence. The irony is the CFO and CBO may be reluctant to discuss the issue amongst them. 

3\. Often the CXOs are egoistic and some resort to politics to prove their superiority. They try to create an atmosphere down below that their directions are the most superior and need to be followed even if not suggested by other CXOs. This creates confusion down below as to whose directions need to be followed. In the above example, the CFO might try to impose his directions on the bidding team, rather than take up the matter with the CBO with the intervention of MD and form a consensus.

4\. Each CXO wants to recruit and strengthen his team in the corporate office though their concerned Departments in various SBUs may have sufficient number of people of similar expertise. For example, the Finance Department of various SBUs may already have a Chartered Accountant (CA), however the CFO may intend to recruit another CA under his belt. Also these recruits, being under CXO and stationed at corporate office, try to flex their muscles to employees of the concerned Departments. This creates a non-cooperation environment between the department and the CXO’s team.

**“Too many CXO”** becomes a common chant amongst employees when they find one after another CXOs being unnecessarily recruited. 

The problem is that all departments need a CXO to be monitored and directed. But how many is the question. There are simple solutions to this problem. 

1. Top Leaders need to combine related functions together to reduce the number of CXO recruits. With lesser number of CXOs, there will be better unity, harmony and clarity of directions in the organisation. For example, Business Development & Marketing, Advertising, Bidding & Costing and Customer Relations can be under the CBO. Finance, Accounts and Audit can be under CFO. Administration, HR, Learning & Development, Wellness department, Unifying Officers, and Governance can be under CHRO. 

2. Under each CXO, there can be different expertise in his corporate team or in the Departments to look after different activities. For example, under CHRO, there could be different Managers either in his corporate team or in the department to look after Administration, HR, Learning & Development, Wellness, and Unifying activities but CHRO remains as the overall single Chief. Also, under CFO, there could be one Chartered Accountant (CA) in his corporate team and it may not be unnecessarily duplicated by recruiting another CA in the concerned Department.  
     
3. Also, different CXOs should meet together to form a consensus for providing same directions to the Department or SBU Heads. For example, the CSO, CFO and the CBO must align together to decide what profit levels the Bidding team should consider to enter an international market for the first time. The profit margin to be considered could be very less if there is huge competition in that market. It could be negative, in other words, bid with anticipated loss to be sure to win, if the market is very big and the presence and learning of the market during operations is of utmost importance. It could be high if the competition is scarce and past results show that the contractors generally bid with high profit margins. There could be different scenarios, but the essence is the CSO, CBO and the CFO must form a common opinion and guide the Department in one direction. 

Let me give you another example. Imagine the pandemonium that would be created if the Cricket Control Board thinks to provide best services in international cricket by recruiting a batting coach, a spin bowling coach, a fast bowling coach, a fielding coach, a wicket keeping coach, etc. Instead there can be a single coach who has a broad knowledge of all the areas of the game. In bowling itself, there are different kinds of bowlers like the off spinners, leg spinners, fast bowling, swingers, googly bowlers, etc. but that does not mean that the Board has to recruit different coaches for different kind of bowling. In batting too, some are proficient in hitting sixes, some in cover drives, and some in sweeping. All these batting abilities require different capabilities, however, irrespective of all these variations, the coach needs to be one or maximum at two who can provide all round required expertise to all the players. This would enable to provide all the players a uniform direction.

Similarly pandemonium will be created if in football there are different coaches like the free kick coach, dribbling coach, penalty shootout coach, goalkeeping coach, mid fielder coach. Isn’t it?

So, in an organisation, **the CXO strength must be kept to a minimum** and the various related activities of the organisation can be clubbed together under a single CXO, as illustrated above.

If you become a Managing Director someday, I wish you be so, please carefully study the business model of your organization thoroughly and discuss with key people to layout your structure of CXOs. Modify them if possible if the structure does not suit with your thoughts or align with the business you are in. Provide each CXO clear roles and responsibilities having no conflict amongst each other. Organise meetings between concerned CXOs if they have different views so that a common final view can be cascaded down below to avoid confusion and provide clarity.

**The salient points are:**

1. Limit your number of CXO recruits  
2. Limit CXO salaries as a maximum percentage of company salary  
3. Engage relevant CXOs to form a consensus on strategic decisions  
4. Eliminate redundancy in positions amongst Departments & CXO’s office.

**CHAPTER : THE “RENOWNED GUY” SYNDROME**

Right person for the right job\! Readers may think what new I am saying. Yes, nothing new. Yet I cannot but state its importance again and again because I have seen organisations faltering in this area.

I have observed that often very renowned personnel are recruited for important positions but eventually they have been found to be non-performers. Why? Simply because the recruit is not the expertise in the particular field even if he is renowned by name. You have to question what are you actually renowned for?

For example, with due respect, Sachin Tendulkar was one of the renowned cricket players of the world but he would have probably performed badly if he was made the Hockey captain of India. Similarly Pele or Maradona were renowned footballers on earth, but they were unlikely to have performed as the Cricket captain of Brazil or Argentina. Am I correct? A Gynecologist and a Cardiologist both may have a MBBS degree and may be renowned, but you cannot expect a Gynecologist to perform a Heart Operation. Such blunders do happen in the corporate. Someone in the industry may be very renowned in Marketing but he cannot perform well if he is put into Operations. Sometimes HR gets swayed away on a person being so renowned, but the question is whether he is the right person for the right job that you are looking for?

Mistakes are committed in another aspect also. Suppose you interview a Procurement officer working in a small sized SBU of a large sized company. The particular SBU may be dealing with few vendors on a small scale of annual procurements. But you get swayed away by the fact that the Procurement officer works in a very large company and thereafter provide him the position of Chief Procurement Officer in your company where you need to deal with hundreds of vendors on a large scale of annual procurements. Can he perform? Definitely not. It’s like asking the pilot of a small propeller jet to fly an A380 Airbus. Imagine the likely fate of the passengers\!

In such a case, the CFO then tries to transfer funds from the SBUs earning well and receiving payments from clients on time to the SBUs incurring losses. By such actions, the Director ruins the business of the SBUs who were performing well. I will explain how.
  3332→
  3333→Assume C1 is the direct cost of materials and services in a project and C2 is the indirect cost, ie. the project management costs including site management costs and overhead costs of the company; then the total cost of the project, say C \= C1 \+ C2. Now a company builds up a net profit, say 7% to build the price. So price P \= 1.07 C. For example if Rs 100 is the total cost of a particular item, the corresponding price is Rs 107 quoted to the client. Remember, in the price of Rs 107, Rs 100 is the cost which is 93.45% of the price (100/107 \= 93.45%).
  3334→
  3335→Assume that the standard client payment terms are (1) 10% Advance for mobilisation against a Advance Bank Guarantee of like amount (2) 80% progressive payment against submission of bills for works done and (3) Retention money of 10% which may be released on completion of the project against a Bank Guarantee of like amount valid till the end of Defects Liability Period (DLP).

  3341→Retention payment \= Rs 10 (10% retention), to be received after completion of the project, as explained above

  3343→Total money received \= Rs 10 (Advance) \+ Rs 80 (progressive) \= Rs 90
